title: Sex Education

jokes.text: How dare you, sir? I was tweeting.
jokes.setup: Judge asks if Tom was texting while driving, expecting denial or justification
jokes.punchline: Tom indignantly corrects that he was tweeting, as if that's better
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Tom's social media obsession makes this distinction feel genuine to him while being absurd to everyone else. Great structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Were you texting while driving?

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Texting? No, your honor, I was tweeting. There's a difference.

jokes.text: 9:17. Drive faster, blue Civic. Daaaaaamn. #soccermoms
jokes.setup: Evidence of Tom's tweets being read in court
jokes.punchline: The specific hashtag and commentary shows his ridiculous priorities while driving
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing Tom's shallow observations. The hashtag feels authentic to the character.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: What is this hashtag '#YoloSwag420'?

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Your Honor, that tweet was sent while I was driving to the courthouse. I was multitasking.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: You were tweeting while operating a vehicle?

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: With all due respect, sir, the hashtag game waits for no man. And neither does traffic on I-96.

jokes.text: Gotta pass this lady on the 'ejkerkj'. That's when I hit the fire hydrant.
jokes.setup: Tom was tweeting while trying to pass someone
jokes.punchline: The gibberish at the end of the tweet marks the exact moment of impact
jokes.explanation: Clever visual storytelling - the keyboard mash perfectly captures the moment of impact. Good structure with clear cause and effect.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: The gibberish at the end of the tweet marks the exact moment of impact

jokes.text: Sorry, allegedly hit the fire hydrant.
jokes.setup: Tom just described hitting a fire hydrant in detail
jokes.punchline: He corrects himself with legal language despite having just provided evidence
jokes.explanation: Decent legal comedy callback but fairly standard. Shows Tom's continued obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Okay, but to be clear, I did not hit that fire hydrant. That fire hydrant was already broken when I got there.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: In fact, I would argue that I actually helped it by giving it a little tap with my car.

jokes.text: #unbreakable. #what'sMr. Glassuptothesedays? whynosequel?
jokes.setup: Tom tweeting about surviving the crash
jokes.punchline: Overly specific movie references in hashtag form show his twisted priorities
jokes.explanation: Good character work but relies on pop culture reference. The multiple hashtags feel authentic to Tom's social media voice.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: #SurvivalInstinct #LuckyToBeAlive #BlessedAndGrateful #JustLikeInTheGreatOutdoorsPartII #ExceptWithoutDanAykroyd #AndWithMorePersonalInjuries

jokes.text: No! Please, send me to jail!
jokes.setup: Judge sentences Tom to a week without screens
jokes.punchline: Tom would literally prefer incarceration to digital detox
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing the depth of Tom's screen addiction. Perfect escalation that feels both absurd and believable for him.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: You are sentenced to one week without any screens or digital devices.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Your Honor, with all due respect, I would literally prefer actual incarceration.

jokes.text: Pr-press send, bailiff! Press send!
jokes.setup: Tom being physically restrained while trying to tweet
jokes.punchline: His desperate pleas to complete one last tweet while being dragged away
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy ending to the sequence. Tom's addiction is so complete he can't stop even when being arrested.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: No, no, no! Just let me finish this tweet! I'm in the middle of a thread! One more second!

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: You don't understand—this is important! My followers need to know about my lunch!

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Stop pulling me! I haven't even added the hashtags yet!

jokes.text: A cowboy hat from your cowboyfriend. That makes it sound like he's a cow.
jokes.setup: Leslie calls Ricky Ann's 'cowboyfriend'
jokes.punchline: Ann points out this makes him sound bovine
jokes.explanation: Basic wordplay. Leslie's tendency to create portmanteaus meets Ann's literal interpretation.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: That makes him sound bovine.

jokes.text: From your cowboy boyfriend. Your boycow-cowboy. Just call him 'Ricky.'
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to fix her portmanteau
jokes.punchline: She makes it progressively worse before giving up
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's inability to admit when her wordplay fails. The escalation works.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Okay, so 'treat yo self' is good, but what if we did 'treat yourself'? No, that's just the full thing. What about 'treat yoself'? Still not working. Okay, 'treayoself'? 'Treatyoself'? 'Tre-yo-self'? I'm just saying random things now.

jokes.text: Great news: Lots of old people have chlamydia. Whoo!
jokes.setup: Leslie announces 'great news'
jokes.punchline: The news is actually terrible - STD epidemic among seniors
jokes.explanation: Leslie's enthusiastic delivery of bad news creates perfect ironic contrast. Her cheerfulness makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I have great news.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: We have an STD epidemic among seniors in Pawnee.

jokes.text: It's amazing what a few old guys can do with a little bit of charm and a lot of crabs.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing the STD situation
jokes.punchline: Double meaning - crabs as STDs and actual crabs
jokes.explanation: Standard double entendre. Leslie's oblivious delivery helps but it's fairly predictable wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I have crabs.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Calm down. I bought them at the seafood market. They're in my office.

jokes.text: They're hopped up on E.D. medication and they got nothing to lose, 'cause they're close to death.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining why seniors are sexually active
jokes.punchline: Blunt assessment that proximity to death makes them reckless
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with Leslie's characteristic bluntness. The logic is sound which makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: When you're that close to death, you stop caring about the rules. You're like, 'What are they gonna do, kill me? I'm already dying!'

jokes.text: Actually, I think it's pronounced 'horny.'
jokes.setup: Someone says seniors can be 'ornery'
jokes.punchline: Andy mishears it as 'horny' which is contextually appropriate
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment - his innocent misunderstanding accidentally lands on the correct interpretation. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Seniors can be ornery.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Horny? I mean, I guess that's... that's great for them.

jokes.text: I'll start. I'm an old lady, why do I need birth control? I haven't had my monthly since LBJ was president.
jokes.setup: April pretending to be an elderly woman asking sex questions
jokes.punchline: Overly specific historical reference for when menopause occurred
jokes.explanation: April's deadpan delivery of gross elderly lady persona works perfectly. The LBJ reference is oddly specific and funny.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: When did menopause start? Was it during the Bronze Age or the Iron Age?

jokes.text: Do pubic hairs get longer the older you get? Because that's happening to me.
jokes.setup: April continuing elderly woman persona
jokes.punchline: Gross overshare delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: Gross-out humor that works because of April's commitment to the bit. The deadpan delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: I haven't felt this good since my last colonoscopy. The doctor said I had the colon of a 25-year-old. I'm not sure what that means, but I've been riding that high ever since.

jokes.text: Where can I get lube that is healthy to eat?
jokes.setup: Tom joining the elderly persona game
jokes.punchline: Disturbing question about edible lubricant
jokes.explanation: More gross-out humor but less clever than April's contribution. Relies mainly on shock value.

jokes.text: I ran over my testicles with my jazzy scooter.
jokes.setup: Andy joining the elderly persona game
jokes.punchline: Impossible physical scenario delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Absurd physical scenario that's both impossible and specific. Andy's innocent delivery makes the bizarre claim funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: I'm 87 years old. My back goes out more than I do.

jokes.text: I want to jump on that caboose. Choo choo!
jokes.setup: Jerry joining the inappropriate elderly questions
jokes.punchline: Inappropriately sexual comment with train sound effect
jokes.explanation: Jerry being inappropriate is funny because it's unexpected, but the joke itself is pretty basic innuendo.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Gergich
dialogue.line: Why did the conductor keep looking back at the passenger car? Because he wanted to see if anyone was getting on... or off. Choo choo!

jokes.text: I'm sorry, sir, but you have to be under 40 to ride this train.
jokes.setup: Jerry's inappropriate train comment
jokes.punchline: Ann's professional response treating it like a real transportation inquiry
jokes.explanation: Ann's professional response to Jerry's innuendo works perfectly. She treats it literally which deflates and enhances it simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Gergich
dialogue.line: I was at the station and saw a train... if you know what I mean.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: Oh, you mean an actual train? Because the station does have excellent service during rush hour. They've really improved the scheduling.

jokes.text: 'LeRon James.' We still on for breakfast?
jokes.setup: Tom showing up late and addressing someone incorrectly
jokes.punchline: He calls someone 'LeRon James' instead of their actual name
jokes.explanation: Tom's tendency to create celebrity nicknames for regular people. Shows his disconnect from reality post-digital detox.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Yo, LeRon James! What's good?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Gergich
dialogue.line: It's Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: I know what I said.

jokes.text: I just drove around in circles until I saw something familiar.
jokes.setup: Tom explaining how he found his way to work without GPS
jokes.punchline: Extremely inefficient navigation method for a 3-mile trip
jokes.explanation: Shows Tom's complete dependence on technology. The absurd solution to a simple problem is very in character.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: I didn't use GPS. I just followed a guy who looked like he knew where he was going. Turned out he was lost too. We ended up at a Arby's in a strip mall. It took us forty-five minutes to go three miles.

jokes.text: I built a real-life Pinterest board.
jokes.setup: Tom claiming he's adapting to life without screens
jokes.punchline: Physical recreation of digital platform, missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom solution - he recreates the digital world physically rather than engaging with actual reality. Shows his fundamental misunderstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: I'm adapting to life without screens. I've created a physical recreation of my favorite digital platform.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: I call it 'Pawnee-Face.' It's just me standing in the town square with a sign that says what I'm thinking, and people walk by and ignore me.

jokes.text: I really wish you could click those.
jokes.setup: Tom looking at his physical Pinterest board
jokes.punchline: He still wants digital functionality from physical objects
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to the Pinterest gag. Shows Tom still doesn't understand why he can't just merge digital and physical.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: I want to pin this to my board, but also have it sync to the cloud.

jokes.text: His name is congressman David Murray, he is our boss and he is white. Don't embarrass me.
jokes.setup: April apparently thinks the white congressman is Obama
jokes.punchline: Ben has to explicitly clarify race to prevent embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Ben's anxiety about April's potential for embarrassment. The specific racial clarification is awkwardly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: April, that's not Obama.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: That congressman is white.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's embarrassing.

jokes.text: And I am April Blart, mall cop.
jokes.setup: Professional introduction to congressman
jokes.punchline: April gives fake name referencing Paul Blart movie
jokes.explanation: Classic April move to sabotage professional situation. The movie reference feels appropriate for her character's trolling.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Paul Blart.

jokes.text: That's right, you could practically cook an egg on the sidewalk.
jokes.setup: Congressman makes small talk about hot weather
jokes.punchline: Ben awkwardly over-agrees with cliche response
jokes.explanation: Ben's nervous over-enthusiasm leads to cliche. Shows his anxiety around important people but the joke itself is weak.

dialogue.speaker: Congressman
dialogue.line: It's pretty hot out there.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: It really is. It's very hot. The heat is... it's a lot. Extremely hot. I would say it's one of the hottest days I can remember. Very, very hot.

jokes.text: Hey, we're still gonna assassinate him, right?
jokes.setup: April asking Ben about strategy meeting
jokes.punchline: She casually suggests assassination in professional context
jokes.explanation: Perfect April moment - casual suggestion of violence in bureaucratic setting. Her tone makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: So what's the strategy for the meeting?

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: We're going to present the budget proposal and address their concerns about the timeline.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: Have you considered just assassinating them?

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: No, April, we've talked about this.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: It would solve a lot of problems.

jokes.text: What the hell is he doing?
jokes.setup: Looking at congressman through window
jokes.punchline: Setting up the reveal that he's just sitting and staring
jokes.explanation: Sets up the congressman's robotic behavior reveal. Functional but not particularly funny on its own.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: He's just sitting there. Staring at nothing.

jokes.text: My. I have two partners, often at the same time.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking seniors about sexual activity
jokes.punchline: Elderly woman casually mentions threesomes
jokes.explanation: Subverts expectations about elderly sexuality. The matter-of-fact delivery from an older woman is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: So, um, how often would you say you're sexually active?

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Woman
dialogue.line: Oh, I'd say three or four times a week. My husband and I, we'll have a threesome.

jokes.text: Heart attack. Falling in love. Partner dies on top of you!
jokes.setup: Leslie asking about risks of unprotected sex
jokes.punchline: Seniors list increasingly dark possibilities beyond STDs
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from medical to emotional to morbid. The elderly perspective on sex risks is darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: What are some of the risks of unprotected sex?

dialogue.speaker: Senior #1
dialogue.line: Well, there's STDs.

dialogue.speaker: Senior #2
dialogue.line: Pregnancy.

dialogue.speaker: Senior #3
dialogue.line: Emotional trauma.

dialogue.speaker: Senior #4
dialogue.line: Your parents finding out.

dialogue.speaker: Senior #5
dialogue.line: A baby that resents you.

dialogue.speaker: Senior #1
dialogue.line: A lifetime of regret and loneliness.

dialogue.speaker: Senior #2
dialogue.line: Death.

jokes.text: I don't know, I thought we were just talking about it.
jokes.setup: Ben asks if she thought seniors were really having sex
jokes.punchline: April's naive assumption that sex education would be theoretical
jokes.explanation: April's deadpan delivery of innocent misunderstanding. Her disconnect from reality is charming here.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: I assumed they would just teach it in theory.

jokes.text: Well, that's all fine and good, but what if the banana is soft and mushy, and doglegs sharply to the left?
jokes.setup: Leslie demonstrating condom use on banana
jokes.punchline: Elderly man describing his penis problems via banana metaphor
jokes.explanation: Clever use of the banana metaphor to describe actual anatomical issues. The specific details make it both gross and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Man
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, mine's more like a plantain at this point. Little darker, a little softer, and it smells like it's been sitting in a bowl for three weeks.

jokes.text: God, Jerry! You don't deserve the Internet!
jokes.setup: Jerry's terrible internet habits
jokes.punchline: Tom's dramatic overreaction about internet worthiness
jokes.explanation: Tom's over-the-top reaction to Jerry's tech incompetence. Good character dynamic showing their different relationships with technology.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Jerry, your internet usage is a crime against humanity. You're streaming cat videos in 480p? That's not just bad, that's a personal attack on everyone with functioning eyeballs.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Gergich
dialogue.line: Tom, it's just the internet—

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Just the internet? JUST THE INTERNET? Jerry, you're literally the only person in this office who doesn't deserve high-speed connectivity. You should have to use dial-up. In 2015. As punishment.

jokes.text: I made an iPhone out of paper. It's not the same, though.
jokes.setup: Tom describing his withdrawal from screens
jokes.punchline: Physical recreation of phone that obviously doesn't work
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual gag showing Tom's desperation. The understated delivery of 'it's not the same' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: I'm doing a physical recreation of my phone. Look, I'm swiping. Nothing's happening. That's the joke.

jokes.text: and her husband, Marshall, is... vivacious.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing the morality watchdog couple
jokes.punchline: Diplomatic way of saying Marshall is flamboyant/eccentric
jokes.explanation: Leslie's diplomatic understatement works well. The pause before 'vivacious' suggests she's searching for the right euphemism.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Marshall is very... expressive. He's a free spirit. He marches to the beat of his own drum. He's not afraid to be himself, you know? He's colorful. He's vibrant. He's—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Look, Marshall is flamboyant, okay? The guy wears a ascot and drives a Prius with vanity plates.

jokes.text: we have babies in thong underwear. Is that what you want?
jokes.setup: Marcia's slippery slope argument about sex education
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous endpoint of babies wearing thongs
jokes.explanation: Absurd slippery slope logic typical of moral panic. The specific image is both ridiculous and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Marcia Langman
dialogue.line: If we teach kids about sex education, it's a slippery slope. Next thing you know, we'll have babies wearing thongs.

jokes.text: Yes, that's what I want.
jokes.setup: Marcia's rhetorical question about babies in thongs
jokes.punchline: Leslie deadpan agrees with the absurd scenario
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response. Leslie's willingness to agree with the absurd accusation rather than dignify it with argument.

dialogue.speaker: Marcia
dialogue.line: Who puts a baby in a thong?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: A baby who doesn't want tan lines.

jokes.text: Andy, pack up our bananas!
jokes.setup: Leslie being forced to end her sex education demonstration
jokes.punchline: Dramatic exit line referencing the banana props
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic exit - the specificity of 'pack up our bananas' makes it sound more important than it is

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Fine! I'm taking my bananas and going home!

jokes.text: I need to get on webMD now! I need a iPhone or a Samsung Galaxy, something with 4G.
jokes.setup: Tom panicking about his splinter
jokes.punchline: His tech addiction overriding his judgment - he needs immediate internet access for a minor injury
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Tom's panic - the specific mention of 4G shows his priorities even in crisis

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I have a splinter. A splinter! Do you know what the mortality rate is for splinters?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I need to look it up. Now. Where's my phone?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I can't remove it without knowing the exact removal procedure. What if I do it wrong? What if it gets infected?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I need WebMD. I need it right now.

jokes.text: It's not my favorite shirt, but it is my least favorite shirt.
jokes.setup: Someone asking April about Ann's cowboy shirt
jokes.punchline: Contradictory statement that technically makes sense
jokes.explanation: Brilliant April logic - technically contradictory but makes perfect sense for her character. Great wordplay structure.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: Ann's shirt is both a cowboy shirt and not a cowboy shirt at the same time. It's a Schrödinger's shirt. Until you observe it, it exists in a superposition of Western and non-Western states.

jokes.text: And 100% of Pawneeans are 'perd-verts.' That's the name I call fans of this show based on the fact that my name is Perd.
jokes.setup: Perd making up statistics about abstinence
jokes.punchline: His awkward self-promotion using a pun on his own name
jokes.explanation: Classic Perd move - over-explaining his own wordplay. The character's complete lack of self-awareness makes it work.

dialogue.speaker: Perd Hapley
dialogue.line: Studies show that 60% of the time, abstinence works every time. I'm Perd Hapley.

jokes.text: Put away your sex toys and play with them on your own time.
jokes.setup: Chris telling Ann to stop the sex education
jokes.punchline: Inadvertent double entendre about playing with sex toys
jokes.explanation: Chris's oblivious innuendo works because of his earnest delivery. He doesn't realize what he's said.

dialogue.speaker: Chris Traeger
dialogue.line: Ann, I need you to stop the sex education class immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: Why? What's wrong with it?

dialogue.speaker: Chris Traeger
dialogue.line: Well, I just got a complaint from a parent saying their child came home talking about 'playing with toys.' I will not have that kind of language in our schools.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: Chris, they're educational toys. It's about demonstrating safe practices.

dialogue.speaker: Chris Traeger
dialogue.line: I don't care if they're made of solid gold. No playing with toys. Not on my watch.

jokes.text: This morning he got in, sat down in there, and has been staring straight ahead, doing nothing.
jokes.setup: Ben asking what the congressman is doing
jokes.punchline: Congressman is literally doing absolutely nothing all day
jokes.explanation: Sets up the congressman's robotic nature. The complete absence of activity is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: What is the congressman doing?

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: Literally doing absolutely nothing all day.

jokes.text: He's not a robot. He's just thinking. He's got a lot on his mind. Yeah, like, 'One, one, zero, one. Must eat babies for fuel.'
jokes.setup: Ben defending congressman, April saying he's a robot
jokes.punchline: April imagines his binary thoughts about consuming babies
jokes.explanation: April's dark robot imagination is perfectly in character. The baby fuel detail is absurd and specific.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: You know he's not a robot, right?

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: Of course he's not a robot.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: His brain is just a computer going '01010101 consume babies consume babies consume babies.'

jokes.text: No, sir. All systems are operational.
jokes.setup: Congressman asking if there's anything else
jokes.punchline: Ben unconsciously responds in robot language
jokes.explanation: Ben unconsciously adopts robotic speech patterns, proving April's point. Good callback to the robot discussion.

dialogue.speaker: Congressman
dialogue.line: Is there anything else?

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: Beep boop beep boop.

jokes.text: Wikipedia: Mankind's greatest invention/ you can learn about anything.
jokes.setup: Tom doing his technology detox confession
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top praise for Wikipedia
jokes.explanation: Tom's hyperbolic praise for basic internet services. Shows his addiction through overvaluation of simple tools.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: I'm doing a technology detox. No phones, no computers, no tablets. I'm going back to basics.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: But I will say, Wikipedia is incredible. It's like having the entire world's knowledge at your fingertips. Every single article is perfect. There are no errors. It's basically the Bible, except it's real and it's better written.

jokes.text: It's actually a guy named Brad that's a teacher in Pittsburgh. We don't have a lot in common, but we chat quite a bit.
jokes.setup: Tom explaining he chats with Brad Pitt
jokes.punchline: It's not the actor, just a random teacher he talks to regularly
jokes.explanation: Tom's random internet friendship is both touching and absurd. Shows his social media connecting him to random strangers.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: I chat with Brad Pitt all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: It's not the actor, just a random teacher I talk to regularly.

jokes.text: There's even a little Indian guy, but he has a turban on, which I think is racist, but the Asian guy also has a racist hat on, and it's like, 'Hold up, didn't Japanese people invent this?'
jokes.setup: Tom explaining emoji
jokes.punchline: His confused racial analysis of emoji stereotypes and attribution
jokes.explanation: Tom's confused cultural commentary feels authentic to his character. The logic is flawed but earnest.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Okay, so emoji are just little pictures, right? But here's the thing - they're racist. Look at this one. This is clearly a Asian businessman emoji. But this one? This is just a regular businessman emoji. So what you're telling me is that one ethnicity of businessman gets special treatment? That's discrimination. And another thing - why is there a taco emoji but no pizza emoji? Oh wait, there is a pizza emoji. Why is there a taco emoji AND a pizza emoji? Because apparently we need to acknowledge every food group, but we can't just have one generic businessman? This is political correctness gone mad.

jokes.text: Jean-Ralphio and I have one called 'Nacho average podcast,' where we rate different kinds of nachos.
jokes.setup: Tom talking about podcasts
jokes.punchline: Terrible pun title for absurdly specific podcast concept
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom and Jean-Ralphio collaboration. Bad pun + hyper-specific food focus = very in character.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm starting a podcast. It's called 'Treat Yo' Shelf: A Podcast About Organizing Your Spice Rack Alphabetically.' First episode: 'Paprika: Am I Doing This Right?'

jokes.text: I had no idea how much you had in your system.
jokes.setup: Ron told Tom to get all the screen addiction out
jokes.punchline: He's overwhelmed by the volume of Tom's digital life
jokes.explanation: Ron's understated reaction to Tom's digital overload. Treats it like drug detox which makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: He's overwhelmed by the volume of Tom's digital life.

jokes.text: I've downloaded every episode of Intervention. I know what to do here.
jokes.setup: Ron saying he knows how to handle Tom's addiction
jokes.punchline: He learned addiction treatment from downloading TV shows
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - Ron uses digital content to cure digital addiction. The specific show reference is perfect for the situation.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I know how to handle Tom's addiction. I learned addiction treatment from downloading TV shows.

jokes.text: Well, maybe not where you come from in 'Tramp-sylvania.'
jokes.setup: Ann saying stopping all sex won't happen
jokes.punchline: Marcia's made-up location suggesting Ann is promiscuous
jokes.explanation: Basic slut-shaming wordplay from morality crusader character. Marshall's 'Good one, honey!' makes it slightly funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Stopping all sex isn't going to happen.

dialogue.speaker: Marcia Langman
dialogue.line: Well, not with that attitude. You're going to give yourself away at the Bone Zone.

jokes.text: I'm from Michigan! That wasn't worth saying.
jokes.setup: Marcia's 'Tramp-sylvania' insult
jokes.punchline: Ann's literal correction followed by dismissal of Marshall's encouragement
jokes.explanation: Ann's practical response to absurd insult, then dismissing Marshall's support. Good character moment showing her directness.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: It's Transylvania, not Tramp-sylvania.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: And Marshall, I don't need your encouragement right now.

jokes.text: You're dressed like an abstinence girl on the outside! God, I'm off my comeback game right now.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to argue with Ann about changing personalities
jokes.punchline: Her weak comeback followed by self-awareness about its weakness
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie self-awareness - acknowledging her comeback is weak makes it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, you're... you're the one who's changing personalities!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know that doesn't even make sense.

jokes.text: You're dressed like Annie Oakley and Pippi Longstocking had a baby and I love it.
jokes.setup: Leslie and Ann arguing about changing for relationships
jokes.punchline: Leslie's specific and sweet description of Ann's cowboy look
jokes.explanation: Leslie's specific pop culture mash-up description is both accurate and affectionate. Shows her friendship despite their fight.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Ann, you can't change who you are for a guy. But if you want to wear that incredible pearl-snap Western shirt with the fringe, and those boots that make your calves look like a young Raquel Welch, then do that for you. Because you look like a sexy ranch owner who also does philanthropy.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: Thank you.

jokes.text: A deer jumped in front of the car, and then a bear jumped in front of the deer!
jokes.setup: Tom explaining why he crashed Ron's car
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd animal excuse
jokes.explanation: Tom's escalating excuse is classic. The bear jumping in front of the deer is absurd enough to be funny.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: I was driving, and a squirrel ran in front of the car, so I swerved to avoid it. Then a raccoon came out of nowhere, so I swerved the other way. Then a deer jumped out, so I swerved again. Then a bear started chasing the car, so I just drove into a tree to be safe.

jokes.text: 'Tommy-edamame' is back on the grid. Tell everyone to light me up with their dijjies, gotta load 'em into my burner.
jokes.setup: Tom buying a new phone against Ron's wishes
jokes.punchline: Tom's ridiculous tech slang and nickname for himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom moment with his tendency to create elaborate slang and nicknames for himself.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yo, yo, yo! It's T-Dawg in the house with my new smartphone. I'm basically a tech genius now.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tom, that's absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ron, you wouldn't understand. I'm about to go viral. That's what the kids call it, right? I'm going to be so viral.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Please stop talking.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: My new name is Tom Haverfard, but you can call me T-Dawg Swag Master 3000. That's my handle.

jokes.text: ♪ I'm Marshall Langman and I'm here to say ♪ ♪ that sex before marriage is never the way ♪
jokes.setup: Marshall performing at sex education event
jokes.punchline: Terrible rap about abstinence
jokes.explanation: Marshall's terrible rap is cringeworthy in the best way. His enthusiasm for bad performance art is very in character.

dialogue.speaker: Marshall Langman
dialogue.line: Yo, yo, yo. My name is Marshall and I'm here to say, I practice abstinence in a major way. I don't have sex, it's not for me, I'd rather watch TV and drink my tea. Abstinence is the way, don't delay, wrap it up or look the other way!

jokes.text: I am not going to be told not to have sex by someone who's ten years my junior.
jokes.setup: Leslie telling seniors to postpone sex until marriage
jokes.punchline: Senior points out age-related absurdity of the advice
jokes.explanation: Senior citizen rebellion against abstinence advice. The age logic is sound and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: You should wait until marriage to have sex.

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Woman
dialogue.line: I'm 87 years old. If I wait until marriage, I'll be dead.

jokes.text: The devil likes to hide in all your private nooks and crannies, and if you open too wide, he might get out, or in.
jokes.setup: Leslie reading from abstinence pamphlet
jokes.punchline: Bizarre sexual metaphor about devil inhabiting body parts
jokes.explanation: Absurd religious sexual metaphor. Leslie's confusion reading it makes it funnier. The logic is completely bizarre.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Okay, so according to this abstinence pamphlet, if you have premarital sex, the devil will inhabit your body parts. Your mouth becomes the devil's puppet. Your hands become the devil's tools. And your genitals become the devil's... playground.

jokes.text: Screw it. Okay, here. This is a penis, right? You put a condom on it...
jokes.setup: Leslie being forced to teach abstinence
jokes.punchline: She snaps and returns to proper sex education
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie moment - she can't help but do the right thing even when it costs her. Her conviction overrides politics.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Okay, fine. You want abstinence education? Here it is: Don't have sex. Because you will get pregnant and die.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Well, not die. But you might get diabetes.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Anyway, here's some contraception pamphlets, a list of local clinics, and my personal phone number if you have questions about safe sex practices.

jokes.text: As your city manager, I recommend that you formally apologize and that, in the future, you stick to the letter of the law, but between you and me, as your best friend... I kind of admire what you did up there.
jokes.setup: Chris giving Leslie official advice
jokes.punchline: He immediately contradicts himself with personal opinion
jokes.explanation: Chris's professional vs. personal split is well-executed. Shows the conflict between official duties and personal values.

dialogue.speaker: Chris Traeger
dialogue.line: As your supervisor, I have to advise you to follow proper protocol and document everything in triplicate.

dialogue.speaker: Chris Traeger
dialogue.line: But between you and me, that's bureaucratic nonsense. Just do what you think is right.

jokes.text: He turns it on when he has to, and when he doesn't, he just sits there. There's no surprises, it's perfect.
jokes.setup: April asking about the congressman's robotic behavior
jokes.punchline: Ben describes it as ideal political behavior
jokes.explanation: Ben's political pragmatism makes robotic behavior sound appealing. Good commentary on political theater.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Why does he move like a robot?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's ideal political behavior. You want to seem human enough that people don't realize you're a lizard person, but robotic enough that they don't expect you to have feelings or opinions.

jokes.text: The truth is I spend a lot of time looking at screens because recently, a lot of the stuff in my real life isn't going that great. So I'd rather play Doodle Jump then think about that.
jokes.setup: Ron asking why Tom needs constant digital distraction
jokes.punchline: Tom's honest admission that screens help him avoid reality
jokes.explanation: Rare moment of genuine vulnerability from Tom. The specific game reference ('Doodle Jump') makes it both funny and relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Why do you need constant digital distraction?

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Because reality is disappointing and my phone is amazing.

jokes.text: I bought a lasso online, Leslie. I turned all my jeans into Daisy Dukes.
jokes.setup: Ann admitting she lost herself in the relationship
jokes.punchline: Specific examples of her cowboy transformation
jokes.explanation: Ann's specific examples of relationship transformation are both absurd and believable. The escalation from lasso to destroyed jeans works.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: I lost myself in that relationship. I was doing things I never thought I'd do.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: I bought a horse. A HORSE. I wore a bolo tie. I went to a gun show... voluntarily.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: I said 'y'all' unironically. Multiple times. In one sentence.

jokes.text: Well, that's good, but I'll tell you, he'll never lasso another heifer as fine as you, Annie Oakley.
jokes.setup: Ann breaking up with cowboy boyfriend
jokes.punchline: Leslie's cowboy-themed compliment calling Ann a heifer
jokes.explanation: Leslie's supportive but inadvertently insulting comparison. Calling your friend a heifer while being sweet is very Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Ann, you are such a beautiful heifer.

jokes.text: Tell that to the folks at Pier 1, an establishment I'm no longer allowed inside.
jokes.setup: Perd saying 'you break it, you buy it' doesn't apply to censure
jokes.punchline: He reveals he's banned from Pier 1 for breaking things
jokes.explanation: Classic Perd non-sequitur. His personal story that doesn't relate to the topic is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Perd Hapley
dialogue.line: You break it, you buy it doesn't apply to censure. I learned that the hard way at Pier 1.

jokes.text: I am giving you a non-electronic book made of paper from a tree.
jokes.setup: Ron giving Tom something to help with screen addiction
jokes.punchline: Over-explanation of what a book is to screen-addicted Tom
jokes.explanation: Ron's over-explanation treats books like alien technology. Perfect for addressing Tom's digital addiction.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: It's a book. Paper. Bound together. Words printed on the pages. You read them. With your eyes. One page at a time. No battery required. No notifications. No algorithm deciding what you read next. You decide. You control the pace. It's just you and the author having a conversation across time.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Does it come in a smaller size? Like for my phone?

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: It IS the smaller size.

jokes.text: No! They got you!
jokes.setup: Ben using robotic congressman phrases
jokes.punchline: April's dramatic realization that Ben has been infected
jokes.explanation: April's horror movie reaction to Ben's robotic language. Good payoff to the running gag about the congressman.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: My fellow Americans, I come before you today to discuss the critical issues facing our great nation.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Oh my God. He's been turned into a robot.

jokes.text: Let's go eat some batteries... I mean, human food.
jokes.setup: Ben suggesting they go eat
jokes.punchline: He accidentally reveals robot programming before correcting himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the robot gag. Ben's slip into robot dietary preferences with quick correction is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: We should go eat. I have been programmed to enjoy consuming nutrients— I mean, I really enjoy eating.

tmdb_overview: An STD outbreak amongst Pawnee seniors prompts Leslie to conduct a sex education class. Meanwhile, Ron helps Tom adjust to life without technology after his gadgets are taken away, and Ben and April meet Congressman Murray for the first time.
insight: Sex education topic generates 70 jokes in 36 minutes, but 76 score suggests uneven landing.
